cisplatin.sourceNotes: Data extracted from FDA package insert (DailyMed setid 00396546-6a80-4b9f-a5f8-c22c1d1bb173); ASHP guidelines provided did not contain cisplatin information.

reconstitution.label: Supplied form
reconstitution.value: Pre-diluted solution: 1 mg/mL cisplatin in sodium chloride, pH 3.8-5.9, ready to dilute (no reconstitution required)

dilution.label: Compatible diluent
dilution.value: 5% Dextrose in 1/2 or 1/3 normal saline

dilution.label: Dilution volume
dilution.value: 2 liters

dilution.label: Additives required
dilution.value: 37.5 g mannitol added to dilution bag

dilution.label: Stability after dilution
dilution.value: Use within 6 hours; if not used within 6 hours, protect solution from light

dilution.label: Important restriction
dilution.value: Do not dilute cisplatin in JUST 5% Dextrose Injection (must contain normal saline component)

administration.label: Route
administration.value: Slow intravenous infusion

administration.label: Infusion duration
administration.value: 6 to 8 hours

administration.label: Administration restriction
administration.value: CISPLATIN SHOULD NOT BE GIVEN BY RAPID INTRAVENOUS INJECTION

administration.label: Frequency
administration.value: Should not be given more frequently than once every 3 to 4 weeks at recommended dosage

administration.label: Equipment restriction
administration.value: Do not use needles or IV sets containing aluminum parts (aluminum reacts with cisplatin causing precipitate formation and loss of potency)

administration.label: Pretreatment hydration
administration.value: 1 to 2 liters of fluid infused for 8 to 12 hours prior to cisplatin dose

administration.label: Post-treatment requirement
administration.value: Adequate hydration and urinary output must be maintained during the following 24 hours

administration.label: Infusion site monitoring
administration.value: Closely monitor infusion site for possible infiltration/extravasation during administration

monitoring.labs: Serum creatinine, BUN, creatinine clearance, magnesium, sodium, potassium, and calcium prior to initiating therapy and prior to each subsequent course
monitoring.duringInfusion: Monitor infusion site for infiltration/extravasation

sideEffects: Nephrotoxicity (dose-limiting, cumulative): renal insufficiency, acute renal failure (28-36% with 50 mg/m² dose), elevated BUN and creatinine, elevated serum uric acid, decreased creatinine clearance, renal tubular damage
Ototoxicity (cumulative, may be severe): tinnitus, high frequency hearing loss, deafness (more pronounced in children)

Peripheral neuropathy: paresthesias in stocking-glove distribution, areflexia, loss of proprioception and vibratory sensation, loss of motor function (may be irreversible, especially with higher doses or greater dose frequencies)
Anaphylactic-like reactions: facial edema, bronchoconstriction, tachycardia, hypotension (may occur within minutes)
blackBoxWarnings: Should be administered under supervision of qualified physician experienced in cancer chemotherapy; adequate diagnostic and treatment facilities must be readily available
Cumulative renal toxicity is severe
Major dose-related toxicities: myelosuppression, nausea, and vomiting
Ototoxicity (more pronounced in children): tinnitus, high frequency hearing loss, occasional deafness
Anaphylactic-like reactions (facial edema, bronchoconstriction, tachycardia, hypotension) may occur within minutes
Exercise caution to prevent inadvertent overdose: doses >100 mg/m²/cycle every 3-4 weeks are rarely used; avoid confusion with carboplatin or daily vs. total dose per cycle
clinicalAlerts: Nephrotoxicity is potentiated by aminoglycoside antibiotics
Elderly patients may be more susceptible to nephrotoxicity and peripheral neuropathy
Do not give repeat course until: serum creatinine <1.5 mg/100 mL, BUN <25 mg/100 mL, platelets ≥100,000/mm³, WBC ≥4,000/mm³
Audiometric testing required prior to initiating therapy, prior to each subsequent dose, and for several years post-therapy in pediatric patients
Contraindicated in patients with pre-existing renal impairment, myelosuppression, hearing impairment, or history of allergic reactions to platinum compounds
Can cause fetal harm; patients should avoid becoming pregnant
Development of acute leukemia has been reported (generally with other leukemogenic agents)
Specific treatment for extravasation unknown

administration.value: Solutions containing cladribine should not be mixed with other IV drugs or additives or infused simultaneously via a common IV line

monitoring.labs: Careful hematologic monitoring, especially during the first 4 to 8 weeks after treatment; monitor platelet count, ANC (absolute neutrophil count), and hemoglobin concentration
monitoring.vitals: Monitor temperature (fever ≥100°F associated with use in approximately two-thirds of patients in first month)

sideEffects: Severe bone marrow suppression including neutropenia, anemia, and thrombocytopenia
Fever (approximately two-thirds of patients in first month)
Neutropenia (47% of patients with ANC ≤1000, including 32% with severe neutropenia ANC ≤500)
Need for RBC transfusions (44% during Month 1)
Need for platelet transfusions (14% during Month 1)
clinicalAlerts: Do not administer live attenuated vaccines to patients receiving cladribine due to increased risk of infection in immunosuppressed patients
Preparations containing benzyl alcohol should not be used in neonates
Physicians should consider delaying or discontinuing drug if neurotoxicity or renal toxicity occurs
Deviations from dosage regimen (0.09 mg/kg/day for 7 days) are not advised
If patient does not respond to initial course, unlikely to benefit from additional courses
Myelosuppressive effects most notable during first month following treatment
Virtually all febrile patients were treated empirically with parenteral antibiotics

administration.label: Dose Titration
administration.value: May double dose at 90-second intervals initially. As BP approaches goal, increase by less than doubling and lengthen intervals to every 5-10 minutes. Approximately 1-2 mg/hour increase produces additional 2-4 mmHg decrease in systolic pressure.

administration.label: Maintenance Dose
administration.value: Most patients achieve desired response at 4-6 mg/hour

administration.label: Maximum Dose
administration.value: Most patients treated with 16 mg/hour or less. Limited short-term experience up to 32 mg/hour.

administration.label: Lipid Load Restriction
administration.value: No more than 1000 mL or average of 21 mg/hour per 24-hour period

administration.value: Little experience beyond 72 hours at any dose

administration.label: Administration Precautions
administration.value: Do not administer in same line as other medications. Invert vial gently several times before use to ensure uniformity. Inspect for particulate matter and discoloration prior to administration.

monitoring.vitals: Monitor blood pressure and heart rate continually during infusion, and then until vital signs are stable
monitoring.duringInfusion: Patients who receive prolonged Cleviprex infusions and are not transitioned to other antihypertensive therapies should be monitored for the possibility of rebound hypertension for at least 8 hours after the infusion is stopped

clinicalAlerts: Contraindicated in patients with allergies to soybeans, soy products, eggs, or egg products
Contraindicated in patients with defective lipid metabolism such as pathologic hyperlipemia, lipoid nephrosis, or acute pancreatitis if accompanied by hyperlipidemia
Contraindicated in patients with severe aortic stenosis
Heart rate increase is normal response to vasodilation; in some patients increases may be pronounced
Risk of rebound hypertension in patients not transitioned to oral antihypertensive therapy after prolonged infusions
Lipid load restriction: no more than 1000 mL or average of 21 mg/hour per 24-hour period
Single-use product: once stopper is punctured, use within 12 hours and discard unused portion

Risk of withdrawal reactions, increased seizure frequency, and status epilepticus with abrupt discontinuation; gradual taper required
Dosage adjustment needed in elderly, CYP2C19 poor metabolizers, and hepatic impairment

clofarabine.sourceNotes: All data derived from FDA package insert (DailyMed) for clofarabine injection; ASHP source mentioned clofarabine only in context of cost savings for outpatient transition but provided no clinical administration or monitoring guidance.

dilution.label: Filtration requirement
dilution.value: Filter through a sterile 0.2 micron syringe filter prior to dilution

dilution.label: Compatible diluents
dilution.value: 5% Dextrose Injection, USP or 0.9% Sodium Chloride Injection, USP

dilution.label: Final concentration range
dilution.value: 0.15 mg/mL to 0.4 mg/mL

dilution.label: Stability after dilution
dilution.value: Use within 24 hours of preparation; store diluted solution at room temperature (15° to 30°C)

administration.label: Route
administration.value: Intravenous infusion

administration.label: Infusion duration
administration.value: 2 hours

administration.label: Schedule
administration.value: Daily for 5 consecutive days of a 28-day cycle

administration.label: Recommended pediatric dose
administration.value: 52 mg/m² based on body surface area calculated using actual height and weight before start of each cycle

administration.label: Line compatibility
administration.value: Do not administer other medications through the same intravenous line to prevent drug incompatibilities

ivCompatibility.label: Other medications
ivCompatibility.value: Do not administer other medications through the same intravenous line

monitoring.labs: Monitor renal and hepatic function during the 5 days of clofarabine injection administration; monitor for signs of clofarabine toxicity when administered with hOAT1, hOAT3, hOCT1 and hOCT2 substrates or inhibitors
monitoring.vitals: Monitor blood pressure in patients taking medications known to affect blood pressure; monitor for hypotension during administration; discontinue if hypotension develops during the 5 days of administration
monitoring.duringInfusion: Monitor cardiac function during administration; monitor for early signs or symptoms of SIRS or capillary leak syndrome (hypotension, tachycardia, tachypnea, and pulmonary edema)

clinicalAlerts: Provide supportive care including intravenous fluids, antihyperuricemic treatment, and alkalinize urine throughout the 5 days of clofarabine administration to reduce risk of tumor lysis and other adverse reactions
Discontinue clofarabine if hypotension develops during the 5 days of administration
Consider prophylactic antiemetic medications as clofarabine is moderately emetogenic
Consider prophylactic steroids to mitigate SIRS or capillary leak syndrome
Minimize exposure to drugs with known renal toxicity during the 5 days of administration
Avoid concomitant use of medications known to induce hepatic toxicity
Reduce dose by 50% in patients with creatinine clearance between 30 mL/min and 60 mL/min
Discontinue if early signs or symptoms of SIRS or capillary leak syndrome occur
Discontinue if Grade 3 or higher increases in creatinine or bilirubin are noted
Withhold for clinically significant infection until controlled
Reduce dose by 25% if Grade 4 neutropenia lasts ≥4 weeks

clonazepam.sourceNotes: Data extracted from FDA package insert (DailyMed); ASHP literature does not contain IV administration or preparation guidance for clonazepam.
blackBoxWarnings: Concomitant use with opioids may result in profound sedation, respiratory depression, coma, and death
Benzodiazepines expose users to risks of abuse, misuse, and addiction, which can lead to overdose or death
Abrupt discontinuation or rapid dosage reduction may precipitate acute withdrawal reactions, which can be life-threatening (e.g., seizures)
clinicalAlerts: Antiepileptic drugs including clonazepam increase the risk of suicidal thoughts or behavior
May cause CNS depression; patients should be cautioned against operating machinery or driving
Use caution in patients with liver disease as hepatic metabolism may be impaired

Avoid contaminating dropper with material from ear, fingers, or other source

administration.value: 50 mL D50W (25 g) co-administered with 10 units regular insulin IV

ivPush.specialConditions: Must use large vein — hypertonic solution causes tissue necrosis if extravasated

monitoring.labs: Monitor blood glucose frequently after administration; monitor serum electrolytes
monitoring.duringInfusion: Monitor infusion site — extravasation of hypertonic dextrose causes tissue necrosis

sideEffects: Hypokalemia — glucose stimulates insulin release, shifting K+ intracellularly
Fluid overload — with repeated large volumes
clinicalAlerts: Must administer via large vein — 50% dextrose is hypertonic (2,525 mOsm/L); causes tissue necrosis on extravasation
Give thiamine BEFORE dextrose in malnourished or alcoholic patients — glucose without thiamine can precipitate Wernicke's encephalopathy
Monitor for rebound hypoglycemia — effect is transient; may need continuous dextrose infusion or feeding
When treating hyperkalemia with insulin: ALWAYS co-administer dextrose to prevent hypoglycemia
NOT for use as a caloric source for TPN — use lower concentrations (D5W, D10W) for continuous infusion
